cards.title: Ads as a profession, not a hobby
cards.front: Why do 90% of advertisers turn Facebook ads into an expensive tax write-off?
cards.back: Treat Facebook ads as a profession, not a hobby. Done well it's a "$1,000-per-hour skill." 90% of advertisers merely dabble, making it an expensive tax write-off. Work means being the chef who samples the soup, not the diner: inject creativity, then measure and track. Never confuse activity with productivity.

cards.title: The core mission: $1 click → $2
cards.front: What is the one-sentence mission that defines profitable Facebook advertising?
cards.back:
Core mission: buy a click for $1, turn it into $2, then make more profit from that $2 than competitors do. It requires four things:
- Understand your sales funnel
- Craft a compelling ad
- A focused landing-page goal
- Automatic tracking/follow-up of leads and customers

cards.title: The grinding mindset (1% edge)
cards.front: How do empires get built on just a penny of profit per click?
cards.back: The grinding mindset: reliably and repeatedly turn a $1 click into $0.01 of pure profit, then scale volume — like pro gamblers "grinding" a 1% edge round after round. "Empires are built on a 1 percent net profit." Don't despise a reliable penny; just make a lot more of them by getting more clicks.

cards.title: Facebook = a pay-for-performance billboard
cards.front: How is a Facebook ad like a billboard that only charges you when it works?
cards.back: Reframe Facebook as a targeted, pay-for-performance billboard: an electronic billboard shown only to your exact audience for free, where you pay ~$1 only when someone takes action — far more efficient than untargeted traditional media (a real billboard cost ~$10,000/month).

cards.title: Eliminate Facebook distractions
cards.front: Before you advertise ON Facebook, what must you do to stop Facebook robbing your own productivity?
cards.back:
Eliminate Facebook distractions to protect productivity:
- Delete the standard Facebook app from your phone (also Twitter, LinkedIn, etc.)
- Realize the world is in a never-ending conspiracy to rob your time, attention, creativity, and mental space
- Close the Facebook tab in your browser
- Entirely stop using Facebook in the usual fashion during work hours
- Block all email notifications from Facebook and other social media
- Halt ALL smartphone notifications — no banners, no red badges
- When you do need Facebook, use the phone browser, not the app

cards.title: Just start: the Facebook easy button
cards.front: What is the lowest-friction first move that beats designing the perfect strategy?
cards.back: Just start — boosting a post is the low-friction first step. Get quick momentum before optimizing strategy by using Facebook's "easy button" rather than designing the perfect strategy out of the gate (or you'll get overwhelmed); refine strategy later. "You don't have to be great to start, but you have to start to be great" (Zig Ziglar).

cards.title: Goals → Content → Targeting sequence
cards.front: In what order should you decide goal, content, and targeting when boosting posts?
cards.back: Goals → Content → Targeting sequence: pick the business goal first, then find matching content, then choose targeting when you boost. Measure organically, stack worthy posts ($1/day × 7 days), then spend more on the winners.

cards.title: Almost every business belongs on Facebook
cards.front: What share of your customers already have a Facebook account — and why does it matter?
cards.back: Almost every business should advertise on Facebook (B2C and B2B): an estimated 80–90% of your customers have Facebook accounts. Supporting numbers: mobile = 85% of FB ad revenue (design mobile-first); the American average spends ~5 hours/day on Facebook; 2 billion people connect monthly.

cards.title: Master the selling system, not the platform
cards.front: After $60M+ in ad spend, what was the #1 lesson — and it isn't about the ad tools?
cards.back: Master the selling system, not just the ad platform. After $60M+ in spend across 100+ accounts, the #1 lesson is that it's about mastering the whole selling system that begins inside Facebook, not the platform mechanics. Treat the ad platform as one piece of a larger funnel/offer/conversion system.

cards.title: Don't live inside Ads Manager
cards.front: Where is the real leverage if it isn't in mastering every dial in Ads Manager?
cards.back: Don't live inside Ads Manager / Power Editor when starting. The leverage ("small hinges swing big doors") is in strategy, audience awareness, offers, and creative — not in mastering every dial. Apply the 80/20 / accelerated-learning approach: master the few high-leverage fundamentals first (like learning the 4 most popular guitar chords to play almost any song), which also makes advanced parts easier later.

cards.title: Your foundation determines your ceiling
cards.front: What single factor sets the ceiling on your ad profit, scalability, and wasted spend?
cards.back: The ad-creation foundation determines your ceiling: "The ceiling of your advertising profit, scalability, and the amount of wasted money swallowed up by Facebook, is directly dependent on the quality of ad creation foundation you build." It's an acquired skill (training + practice + tools + system + guide), not raw talent.

cards.title: The Facebook Flight Plan
cards.front: Why does a former pilot say profitable Facebook ads are learned exactly like flying?
cards.back: The Facebook Flight Plan is the book's organizing mental model: profitable advertising is learned like flying — through training, systems, SOPs, checklists, and redundancy (a "Facebook Flight School" with key lessons, normal procedures/checklists, abnormal procedures/checklists, and flight-planning guides). Core philosophy: master fundamentals before complexity — start in a single-engine Cessna with an instructor, not a Boeing 747. Best early advice: keep it simple and gain experience.

cards.title: Flight Plan stages and milestones
cards.front: What conversion milestones gate the stages of the Flight Plan?
cards.back:
Flight Plan stages:
- Stages One & Two — build the foundation: a Like Campaign, a Link Post Ad, a Short-Copy Ad, and a Long-Copy Ad
- Stage Three — entered only after 1,000+ conversions at acceptable cost/ROI (hooks and offers already tested); adds two layers: (1) Video Ads and (2) Retargeting for Hot Prospects, with Dynamic Product Ads (DPAs) at the very end
Milestone: generating your first 100+ conversions is the hardest part of the whole journey (like the first two minutes of a shuttle launch); once past it, the path gets easier. Counts are guidelines, not rigid rules.

cards.title: Why one audience needs many ad types
cards.front: Why can't a single ad serve everyone inside one targeted audience?
cards.back: Any single audience contains people at different awareness levels, intent levels, trust/rapport levels, and buyer personas — all needing different messaging, and many people must see your ads repeatedly before acting. That's why you layer on more ad types as you progress through the Flight Plan.

cards.title: Like campaigns = the beginner's flight simulator
cards.front: What low-risk campaign lets a beginner practice with real data and no funnel?
cards.back:
Like campaigns are the beginner's "flight simulator" — low-risk learning with no landing pages, thank-you pages, conversion pixels, or tracking needed. Three benefits beyond education:
- Social proof — friends-of-fans targeting makes ads look recommended (the "restaurant with a line" effect)
- Fans see your ads ~2× more — non-fans capped at 2 newsfeed views/day, fans up to 4/day (right column has no frequency cap)
- Build warm audiences — conversion campaigns to quality fans yield the best ROI

cards.title: Intent vs. Interruption marketing
cards.front: Why does a plumbing ad that crushes it on Google bomb on Facebook?
cards.back: Intent vs. Interruption: Google/YouTube searchers show active intent, so direct solution ads work (e.g., "We fix broken water pipes, there in 30 minutes"). Facebook is interruption marketing — no one is looking for a solution; they want an escape. The same plumbing ad bombs on Facebook. AdWords skills don't carry over.
cards.example: A solution-specific plumbing ad that wins on Google flops on Facebook because no one there is searching for a plumber.

cards.title: Three Business Categories
cards.front: Why does copying someone else's funnel usually fail — and which three buckets must you choose between?
cards.back:
Nearly all Facebook advertisers fall into one of three Business Categories, each needing a DIFFERENT selling system (they differ in offer, inventory, pricing, margins, frequency, competition, costs, and trust required):
- Retail, Local, and Service Businesses
- eCommerce and Physical Products
- Digital and Information Products (including SaaS)
Big mistake: most businesses copy a model built for information marketers. With interruption marketing there is no one-size-fits-all funnel — identify your category, use the proven system, then tweak. Trust scales with ask size: cheat-sheet (low) → free assessment requiring travel (medium) → $70 credit-card purchase (high).

cards.title: Match value/trust to the size of the ask
cards.front: How should your ad's value change between a cheat-sheet opt-in and a credit-card sale?
cards.back:
Match value/trust to the size of the ask:
- Minor ask (cheat-sheet opt-in) = simple image link post, short copy, video optional
- Bigger ask (webinar 90–120 min) = pre-commit with a short clip + long copy
- Highest ask (free+shipping requiring a credit card; product sales page) = strong upfront value via an educational video that elevates prospect status first

cards.title: The Customer Buying Cycle
cards.front: What subconscious emotional six-step process does every purchase follow?
cards.back:
Every purchase follows this Customer Buying Cycle:
- Problem — a problem you want solved (e.g., looking good for a reunion)
- Research / Interest — you research (Google, Amazon reviews, ask friends)
- Evaluate options — done emotionally, not logically ("how will this make me feel?")
- Justify with logic — "the mind will justify what the heart has already decided" (Roy H. Williams)
- Purchase
- Brand Ambassador — if they love it they become a raving fan and tell others; this is where you strive for all customers to end up

cards.title: UPSYD: five levels of audience awareness
cards.front: What does the acronym UPSYD stand for, and where on the staircase should you start?
cards.back:
UPSYD (pronounced "upside") reverses Eugene Schwartz's five awareness levels (Breakthrough Advertising, 1966):
- U = Unaware — don't realize they have a problem
- P = Problem Aware — recognize the problem, don't know what to do
- S = Solution Aware — know solutions exist, not which is right, don't know your product
- Y = Your Solution Aware — know your product, haven't purchased yet
- D = Deal — just need a great deal to purchase
Goal: perpetually move people up the staircase from awareness to intent. Start at Level 3 (Solution Aware), build momentum, then expand outward. Targeting only "Your Solution Aware" people gets easy sales but can't sustain a business — the audience is too small.
cards.example: Ketogenic Living used UPSYD + broad messaging on a from-scratch brand to get 29,000+ fans and 5,000+ leads in 1 month.

cards.title: Four definitions of an ad "working"
cards.front: "Is my ad working?" has four answers — but Facebook only measures the first. What are they?
cards.back:
Four progressively deeper measures of an ad "working" (Facebook directly answers only the first, via CTR / clicks):
- Does the ad encourage users to click?
- Will users do the next step you want after clicking?
- Will users ever purchase?
- Will users stop buying from the competitor?

cards.title: Audience-First (James) vs You-First (Ricky)
cards.front: Two identical-twin guitar teachers, opposite results — which mindset wins and why?
cards.back:
Two mindsets dramatized as twins:
- Ricky (you-first) showcases his own 10,000-hour mastery, leans on referrals + discounts, frames mastery as a daunting climb. Result: admiration but zero conversions.
- James (audience-first) teaches a quick shareable win (four chords ≈ any song), lets the prospect DO it and succeed, frames mastery as achievable shortcuts, offers a low-friction lead magnet. Result: mile-long waiting list, 12 trained instructors, instant opt-ins.
Lesson: make the audience the star/hero, not yourself. "He wasn't the star of the show — his audience was the star." Be like James.

cards.title: Strategy & messaging beat targeting
cards.front: Why does getting messaging right beat even perfect targeting and ninja optimization?
cards.back: Strategy & messaging beat targeting & ninja optimization. Even with perfect targeting you can't know a prospect's top-of-mind problem state. Facebook is really about getting messaging right — understanding the conversations in people's minds and meeting them where they are. Over-focusing on targeting data / hyper-targeting & retargeting is the biggest mistake; it makes people miss the big picture.

cards.title: Getting the offer/messaging right is THE key
cards.front: What single takeaway means you can't scale no matter how much of a "Facebook ninja" you are?
cards.back: Getting the offer/messaging right is THE key — the single most important takeaway. Without a winning offer/message you can't scale no matter how much of a "Facebook ninja" you are; tactics like DPAs are secondary.

cards.title: Right audience + great hook = one-two punch
cards.front: Per Perry Marshall, what beats all sales, copy, and negotiation techniques combined?
cards.back: Right audience + great hook/offer = a one-two punch. Per Perry Marshall, selling to the RIGHT PERSON beats all sales/copy/negotiation techniques (the wrong person has no money, doesn't care, won't be persuaded). A great hook/offer can win even with sloppy targeting; tightening targeting on top super-charges it.

cards.title: Cold vs retargeting split (90/10)
cards.front: What share of a profitable campaign should be cold traffic vs retargeting?
cards.back: Don't get caught up on retargeting — hook and offer matter most; retargeting is just "icing on top." Profitable campaigns run ~90% cold traffic and ~10% retargeting.

cards.title: One good ad can power the whole system
cards.front: Why can a single winning ad generate fans, leads, and customers all at once?
cards.back: One good ad can power the whole system — simultaneously generating fans, leads, and customers while building brand goodwill, and making it much easier to scale without conversion costs skyrocketing. A winning long-copy ad can later become the storyboard for a video ad.

cards.title: Research is the key to a great hook
cards.front: If the greatest ad man spent 3 weeks on one car manual, how long should YOU research a hook?
cards.back: Research is the key to a great hook — don't copy competitors outright, but don't ignore them (a huge source of ideas). Ogilvy spent 3 weeks on the Rolls-Royce manual; his Mercedes work increased U.S. sales 400% in one year (10,000 → 40,000 cars). If the greatest ad man spends 3 weeks, you can spend a few hours. If fulfillment falls short of the hook's promise, the hook stops working.

cards.title: Four research methods to hack the hook
cards.front: What are the four research methods used to hack a winning hook?
cards.back:
Four research methods to hack the hook:
- Google
- Surveys
- Interviews
- Facebook
Survey the hook with open-ended, specificity-forcing questions to find hidden patterns (use SurveyMonkey). Interview the hook by narrowing to your BEST avatar, then interviewing that group directly (e.g., inside a relevant Facebook group) about fears, desires, and ideal solutions.
cards.example: Bob's mail-order-bride business found 40% of customers were truckers; the authors found ~40% were consultants/agencies, which spawned a certification program.

cards.title: Hook-hacking survey & avatar questions
cards.front: What verbatim questions surface a customer's real frustration and your best avatar?
cards.back:
Survey question (verbatim): "What's your number one single biggest challenge or frustration with [X] right now? (Please be as specific as possible. Please go beyond saying 'too confusing' or 'too expensive.' The more specific and detailed you are, the more likely I'll be able to cover your topic :-)"
Motivation question: "Why do you want to grow your business using Facebook ads or any online channel? What is the biggest reason...?"
Avatar-narrowing questions: "Who is your customer? Which one is your BEST customer? Why are they your best customer?"
Solution-elicitation interview question: "If there was a solution to this problem, what would it look like?"

cards.title: Focus only on buyers (avatar shift)
cards.front: What happened when one business killed its free lead magnets and targeted only buyers?
cards.back: Buyer-only avatar shift: killing free lead magnets and focusing only on buyers produced ~3× revenue and cut customer-service requests by ~⅔. Targeting your best (paying) avatar concentrates effort where it pays off.

cards.title: The 7 Power Questions
cards.front: How many of the 7 Power Questions must you nail to beat most competitors?
cards.back: Run the 7 Power Questions before finishing any copy/ad. You don't need all seven — nailing even one or two effectively makes your marketing far more powerful, surfaces hooks you previously missed, and beats most competitors. The ideal is to answer all seven well. Caveat: this amplifies attention, so the underlying product/service must actually be good.

cards.title: Focused attention is the prerequisite
cards.front: Why won't even a great product convert if you skip this one prerequisite?
cards.back: Focused attention is the prerequisite for conversion — don't assume people will read/watch. The job of copy is to make your brand/message focal and salient; if they don't pay attention, it won't convert regardless of product quality. Live in the "is" world, not the "should be" world: accept that ~95% of users are in mild "hypnosis" chasing Likes/Shares, and use it.

cards.title: Environment reset (Pavlov's new room)
cards.front: Why does moving a prospect through a "portal" make them more likely to decide anew?
cards.back: Environment reset (Pavlov's "new room"): trained dogs stopped salivating at the bell in a new room — changing environment causes a mental reset. Moving a person through a "portal" makes them more likely to make a new decision (cited via Cialdini's Pre-Suasion).

cards.title: Video ads beat image ads long-term
cards.front: Why is an image ad a "race for fools" while video is the marathon?
cards.back: Long-term video ads beat short-term image/direct-response ads. Image ads are a "sprint" that only works on already-primed buyers and dies in ~7–10 days ("a race for fools" — Roy H. Williams). Video ads are "the steps that make up the marathon" — like radio/TV they run profitably for 6 months+ (some agency client videos for 1–2 years+). Goal: a "set it and forget it" campaign. Does NOT require high production value — an iPhone mic is broadcast-quality enough; the most vital action is just to make a video.

cards.title: Guard-up vs guard-down entry
cards.front: What question must you ask before launch about how a prospect enters your funnel?
cards.back: Guard-up vs guard-down entry: image-only leads arrive with their guard up, expecting to be sold; video-first leads arrive guard down, pre-sold, with "your brand in their hearts and minds." Always ask before launch: "How is that person coming into our sales funnel?" Applied to cold traffic, an educational video ad takes prospects "from a state of unawareness all the way up the UPSYD ladder" before they hit the sales page (educate → elevate status → present product as the next logical solution).

cards.title: Creative = being a good marketer
cards.front: What single question turns "being creative" into being a good marketer?
cards.back: Creative = being a good marketer. Always ask: "How can I convey a marketing message that will get someone to do what I want them to do?" (Recommended reading: Elizabeth Gilbert, Big Magic.)

cards.title: Pre-flight diagnostics before any campaign
cards.front: What six diagnostic checks should you run before launching any campaign?
cards.back:
Pre-flight diagnostics before any campaign:
- Identify which of the three Business Categories you fall into and use that category's proven selling system
- Map your target audience across the UPSYD awareness levels
- Decide where on the UPSYD staircase to start (default: Level 3, Solution Aware)
- Match the value/trust in your ad to the size of the ask
- Ask: "How is that person coming into our sales funnel?" (guard up vs guard down)
- Run the 7 Power Questions on the copy before finishing the ad

cards.title: Checklists & a living Ad Creation SOP
cards.front: What hospital-proven habit makes ad operations repeatable and scalable?
cards.back: Checklists reduce costly mistakes — per Atul Gawande's The Checklist Manifesto, hospitals adopting airline-style checklists had dramatically lower error rates. Build checklists/SOPs into ad operations for repeatable, scalable results, and maintain a living Ad Creation SOP, updated monthly, because the platform changes constantly.

cards.title: Paint-by-numbers templates burn out
cards.front: Why do copy-and-paste ad templates work briefly then betray you?
cards.back: Pitfall: copy-and-paste / paint-by-numbers templates burn out fast. They may work briefly but fade and require constant rotation. Aim to build a "masterpiece" and to become an architect/engineer who understands WHY it works — skills that transfer to any channel. Don't obsess over ad/ad-set counts, image/copy versions, or audience size before building the foundation.

cards.title: Don't buy cheap bulk likes
cards.front: Why does buying cheap bulk likes actually kill your organic reach?
cards.back: Pitfall: don't buy cheap bulk likes off-Facebook — a high ratio of non-engaging fans kills organic reach (Like-campaign Myth #3). Sloppy/broad targeting also produces poor-quality leads (Myth #2).

cards.title: Advertise responsibly
cards.front: What obligation comes with mastering persuasion at scale?
cards.back: Advertise responsibly — "with great power comes great responsibility." Mastery of persuasion at scale obligates you to avoid manipulative or harmful offers. Also don't expect overnight mastery or treat ads as set-and-forget to learn: running profitable, scalable campaigns is genuinely hard (the author says harder than learning to fly). Commit to systematic learning and experience-building.
